subject: Joint Distributions
topic: §42 Sum of Two Independent Random Variables: Continuous Case
question: Let $X$ and $Y$ be two independent random variables uniformly distributed on $[0, 1]$. Compute the probability density function of $X + Y$.
explanation: Since $f_X(a) = f_Y(a) = \begin{cases} 1 & 0 \le a \le 1 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ by the previous theorem $f_{X+Y}(a) = \int_0^1 f_X(a - y)\,dy$. Now the integrand is 0 unless $0 \le a - y \le 1$ (i.e. unless $a - 1 \le y \le a$) and then it is 1. So if $0 \le a \le 1$ then $f_{X+Y}(a) = \int_0^a dy = a$. If $1 < a < 2$ then $f_{X+Y}(a) = \int_{a-1}^1 dy = 2 - a$. Hence, $f_{X+Y}(a) = \begin{cases} a & 0 \le a \le 1 \\ 2 - a & 1 < a < 2 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$
